papers.title: WHTZ 1: A high excitation Planetary Nebula not a gaseous cocoon from runaway star HD 185806
papers.author_names: Quentin A. Parker Pascal Le Dû Andreas Ritter Peter Goodhew Sakib Rasool Stephane Charbonnel Olivier Garde Lionel Mulato Thomas Petit

abstract.text: We present evidence that the nebular cocoon and bow-shock emission nebula putatively and recently reported as deriving from the 9th magnitude "runaway" star HD 185806 is the previously discovered but obscure planetary nebula WHTZ 1 (Ra 7). It has a Gaia DR3 G~16 blue ionizing star at its geometric centre. We present imagery, spectroscopy, other data and arguments to support that this emission source is a high excitation Planetary Nebula not a stellar wind bow shock.

brief.tldr: [astronomical reclassification study] The paper reclassifies the previously reported bow-shock nebula associated with runaway star HD 185806 as the known planetary nebula WHTZ 1 (Ra 7), using multi-wavelength imaging and spectroscopy to demonstrate that the emission arises from a high-excitation planetary nebula with a blue ionizing central star, not from a stellar wind shock. The reassignment is supported by historical records, morphological consistency, spectral features, and archival data, resolving a misattribution that had persisted in recent literature.

source_fields.title: WHTZ 1: A high excitation Planetary Nebula not a gaseous cocoon from runaway star HD 185806

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: The cocoon "bow-shock" nebula previously associated with HD 185806 is actually the evolved planetary nebula WHTZ 1, with the bright star HD 185806 being a foreground chance projection, not the ionizing source. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of re-establishing the original planetary nebula classification of WHTZ 1 (formerly misidentified as a bow-shock cocoon from HD 185806) by combining historical context and observational evidence, thereby solving the problem of persistent misidentification and re-assignment of celestial objects in the face of evolving data and interpretations. Methods and Observations: The paper proposes a multi-wavelength observational approach combining deep amateur narrow-band imaging (Hα and [OIII]) with spectroscopic and positional analysis to confirm WHTZ 1 as a true high-excitation planetary nebula by demonstrating its distinct emission characteristics and ruling out contamination from a nearby ionizing star like HD 185806. Broader Geometric considerations: Geometric and multi-wavelength analyses strongly support the blue central star as the true ionizing source of the nebula, contradicting bow-shock interpretations due to the lack of observed features consistent with known runaway star bow shocks. Corollaries with other known PNe: WHTZ 1 shares morphological and structural features with other known planetary nebulae, such as elliptical shapes, enhanced edges, central blue CSPNe, and proximity to bright stars, demonstrating a common pattern among these objects. Dust and extinction in the local environment: The local environment around WHTZ 1 is characterized by low dust content and minimal extinction, resulting in strong [OIII] emission relative to Hα due to the lack of interstellar medium obscuration. Summary of observed properties and assessment: The observed properties—morphology, stellar association, spectral features, kinematics, and spatial alignment—strongly support the identification of WHTZ 1 as a high-excitation planetary nebula rather than a bow shock from a runaway star. Distance estimates and Kinematic age for the nebula: The distance to PN WHTZ 1 is consistent between PN statistical methods and Gaia parallax, yielding a kinematic age of approximately 25,000 years, which is typical for planetary nebulae despite being at the higher end for such a faint and evolved object.

title: WHTZ 1: A high excitation Planetary Nebula not a gaseous cocoon from runaway star HD 185806

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents follow-up observations (imagery, spectroscopy) of a specific star HD 185806 and nebula WHTZ 1 to reclassify the object
